title: Globally elevated greenhouse gas emissions from polluted urban rivers
data_sources: Urban river GHG compiled dataset (5090 measurements, figshare)

data_scope.ghg_measurements: 5090 individual in situ measurements of CH4, CO2, N2O concentrations and fluxes
data_scope.climate_coverage: 94-99% of world urban climatic conditions (T: 5.8-28.6 C; P: 233-2835 mm/yr)
data_scope.spatial_resolution: Reach-level (~3 million global river reaches in GRADES)

core_conclusion: Urban rivers globally emit 78.1 +/- 3.5 Tg CO2-equivalent per year (1.1 Tg CH4, 42.3 Tg CO2, 0.021 Tg N2O), at nearly twice the emission rate of non-urban rivers and comparable in intensity to direct urban anthropogenic activities. CH4 and N2O are disproportionately elevated due to eutrophication and anoxia from urban pollution. Emissions peak in lower-middle-income countries, following an Environmental Kuznets Curve, and controlling river pollution offers a co-benefit pathway for both water quality restoration and urban climate mitigation.
why_it_matters: First global GHG inventory for urban rivers closing a major gap in city-scale climate accounting; reveals disproportionate CH4 and N2O signature (urban rivers ~0.8% of global river area but ~15.4% of riverine N2O emissions); links income inequality to GHG vulnerability; demonstrates pollution-climate co-benefit; provides a globally transferable Random Forest predictive model.
why_nature: Planet-scale synthesis with direct SDG 6/11/13 policy relevance; integrates ecological, climate, and development-economics framing (Environmental Kuznets Curve); open data and reproducible science; actionable co-benefit framing for sustainable urban development strategy.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatially explicit wastewater-facility siting optimization: adapt my EV-charging efficiency-equity framework to minimize CO2-eq per unit remediation cost across the 1,566 MUAs

Spatiotemporally explicit SHAP/MGWR attribution of GHG drivers: extend the paper's global RF model to geographically varying importance maps, demonstrating non-stationarity in driver importance across income and climate zones

Intra-city pollution-burden accessibility: adapt 2SFCA/R2SFCA to quantify which population sub-groups live within GHG-flux-weighted buffers of polluted rivers, linking river remediation to health equity

SSP-scenario urbanization projections: feed the paper's GDP/population predictors with SSP trajectories to generate 2030-2050 urban river GHG forecasts, using the same spatiotemporal simulation methods I apply to city-region analysis

Active learning for sparse environmental monitoring: design targeted sampling strategies using the paper's uncertainty maps to augment data in low-income cities, paralleling my geospatial foundation model fine-tuning approach

related.title: Urban river pollution control in developing countries
related.relation: empirical evidence for restoration effectiveness and pollution control strategies in urban rivers

related.relation: population/density scaling of urban GHG emissions, relevant to socio-economic predictors and future projections

related.title: Managing urban development could halve nitrogen pollution in China
related.relation: nitrogen pollution management through urban development control, directly relevant to N2O emission mitigation from urban rivers

related.relation: urban methane monitoring and hidden emission sources, relevant to data gaps and mobile sensing extension
